result:
The Dripping Paragraph.

There is a small café on Rue des Jardins where the espresso tastes like rain hammered for centuries against copper roofs and then distilled into one glossy, black drop. I am sitting in its tin-roof annex, watching a single drop trace its way down the window, elongating, quivering like it’s carrying the entire Atlantic in its belly. When it finally lets go, it leaves an embryonic smear on the glass—evidence that something, somewhere, has moved on. I capture the smear with my phone, knowing I’ll delete it in a week, delete even the deletion, because memory never likes being hoarded like loose buttons in a jar. It was never about keeping; it was about noticing I once could.

Outside, a woman carries a purple umbrella shaped like a bishop’s hat. Nothing about her is symmetrical: the umbrella tilts, her shoulders list to one side, and the pigeons refuse to part evenly for her. She looks like a handwritten note that got folded one too many times. Still, she stops at the bookstore opposite, shakes the rain off into a perfect half circle, and disappears between shelves that smell of worm-eaten wisdom. I wonder what page she will breathe in first—maybe Neruda, maybe an appliance warranty. Either way, she will hear a faint click inside her chest: someone else has felt this, has survived.

A sudden metallic clang behind me—La Patrone has dropped her tray. One shattered cup. The espresso ghosts itself across the tiles, turning the beige floor briefly into the Milky Way at 4 a.m. Black stars, white void. No one gasps; the regulars merely lift their ankles like flamingos until the constellation is wiped away. The drag of the rag becomes the loudest sound in the universe. Somewhere in that sound is everything we are embarrassed to admit: how fragile we are in our gladness, how quickly the cosmos doubles as a janitor with a dull green mop.

La Patrone glances at me, mutters “Encore un,” and pulls two fresh espressos. She presses one toward me as recompense for witnessing the collapse of porcelain. I say thank you in the almost-French of a trespasser who loves vowels more than visas. She shrugs: _du gâchis à l’attention_, waste taken personally. I sip. The ocean inside the drop slams into my veins, and for one thudding second I can name every island I haven’t visited. The temptation is to list them aloud—Svalbard, Tuvalu, Neverland—but islands prefer anonymity. So I keep silent. Silence is currency here.

The rain changes gear, now a slanted martinet drumming on umbrellas, roofs, the encyclopedic puddles. Each puddle is a bas-relief of the city turned upside-down: cathedral spires like knitting needles driven upward into heaven’s throat, neon Somewhere signs melted into lowercase, tram lines reimagined as piano strings still humming Chopin. In one puddle’s convex eye I see my reflection kneeling. This shocks me because I am sitting. The extra me must be some doppelgänger who has learned how to pocket his despair and kneel without bruising. I wave. He nods—obliging but not encouraging—then fragments into concentric ripples caused by a passing stroller. The child inside grips a plastic dinosaur and regards me as if I’m the fossil. I probably am. Extinction just hasn’t finished its paperwork.

The bookish woman re-emerges sans umbrella. She holds the book high against the rain as though literature were a modest shield. The purple umbrella lies by the door like a fallen monarch. I understand. Sometimes we abandon even the necessary objects when we find another armor. As she limps away, I spot the cover: the collected poems of Audre Lorde. Sunlight, even borrowed, even paper-screened, daggers straight into my solar plexus. I inhale once, twice. It does not feel like breathing; it feels like preparing.

I decide I will speak to her. Not because of romance (my heart is already a city under curfew) but because language needs witnesses. I pay. The register rings like a bicycle bell centuries ago on a street that no longer exists, and I step into rain that now behaves like applause heard underwater. My jacket darkens; my hair wilts into seaweed captions. She is two blocks ahead, haloed by the intermittent flicker of a traffic light stuck in perpetual yellow.

The sky at this moment is a bruised forgery of dusk, although the clock insists it’s barely noon. From an unseen apartment comes the scented chord of someone cooking onion and orange rind—an improbable duet that collides midair with rain and becomes the smell of nostalgic revolutions. I walk inside that smell as though it is a corridor. At the corridor’s end stands the woman, having paused beneath a chestnut tree that rains its own private storm of leaves. Spent leaves stick to the pages of the Lorde; glossy, skeletal bookmarks.

“Excuse me,” I begin, every consonant a wet match trying to strike. “I saw you leave the café with that book. Lorde saved my life once by teaching me I could save my own.”

She turns, and her eyes are neither startled nor unsurprised—they are red-sleeved nights in which emergency exits glow. “Saved mine too,” she says simply. “Twice a week, like chapel.” Then the small shared hush that is itself a stanza break.

The leaves keep falling, practicing small surrenders. An elderly man with a dog shaped like a question mark passes by; the dog sniffs my shoelace as if certain it once belonged to a king. The man smiles at us like we’re thatched into the same folklore and continues, muttering numbers: 1942, 7.3, never again. Rain softens them into lullabies.

She tells me her name: Amarine. Says her mother liked to mispronounce “amaranth” until it sounded like something you could sail. I confess I once kept dried amaranth in a drawer; when the petals finally dropped they wrote tiny SOS messages across the cedar. We laugh; the laughter is identical to steam rising from a subway grate—there, then infinitely far away.

“I’ve never finished reading her,” I admit, nodding at the coral-spined book. “Each poem feels like I’m writing it with my whole body, and halfway through I need to rest.” Amarine’s hand grazes mine as we both hold the Lorde; the book is warm where her palm was, cold where mine appears. Between our fingerprints is the latitude where warmth meets chill and neither apologizes.

Time hangs like a wet coat between us; somewhere behind,alm trees, a church bell rings thirteen amateurish times, as though counting something only partially possible. Amarine says she must catch a train at Gare des Minimes at twelve-thirty, which already occurred thirteen bells ago. Yet trains, like hours, barely keep their own promises. “On va quand même,” she says. We go anyway.

Gare des Minimes is ten blocks east, all uphill. The rain eases into punctuation marks—apostrophes, commas, the rare exclamation point when traffic hisses by. We walk not briskly, not lazily, but ceremonially, like custodians returning overdue relics. At one intersection, kids in red slickers dance across the zebra stripes; one drops a paper boat that immediately sinks, but before it does, I swear it spells “amen” in Morse of ripples.

By the time we reach the station, the sun—a coy narrator—has edited out the storm. Steam rises off the pavement in pale gymnastic tumbles. The iron ribs of the station smell rusted and nostalgic, like grandfathers opening biscuit tins. Announcements echo in French and something that might as well be extinct. Amarine’s train—the 12:32 to somewhere vineyarded—exhales.

She steps onto the train but pivots back, pressing the Lorde into my chest. I protest: an unfinished book. She smiles, a crescent made of absences. “That’s the point. Finish it aloud for strangers until it finishes you.” The doors fold her away like a secret reluctantly kept. I stand there holding a whole forest of unclaimed syllables.

The train leaves, and the station resumes its hollow heartbeat. I open the book; petals of old amaranth fall out, dehydrated but bright, like suns that ran away from their galaxies. On the flyleaf, a single sentence in faint blue ink: “When the small sounds return, answer.” I pocket the petals as if they are train tickets to every somewhere I am still afraid to pronounce.

Outside, the sky is clear, vulgarly beautiful. Somewhere, I imagine Amarine is writing this same scene from the opposite window: man standing in steam, holding a book leaking crimson petals, looking both newly born and newly ruined. Between us blooms a mutual invisible garden watered by rain that no longer falls yet is still present, the way unfinished poems are present—in the mouth’s tremble before vowels surrender.

I begin to walk back the way we came. The puddles have evaporated, but if I bend low I can still see their negatives—darkened silhouettes on the asphalt, ghost mirrors making no demands. One of them carries the whisper of a cathedral spire touching its own infinity. I kneel, letting the book rest an inch above the silhouette, like a lid on possible worlds.

A child passes, maybe seven, dragging a broken ukulele by one string. She pauses, curious. I open the page at random and read in a cracked half-voice: “I dream of a place between no and yes.” The child grins, strums the broken string—sound like a hinge—and wanders off humming.

The ukulele’s solitary twang follows me all the way home—if cafés and borrowed chapter headings can be called home. It is the sound a horizon might make when snapping its own sternum apart to reveal softer blue beneath. I do not know where the horizon stores its tenderness, but tonight I believe it has entrusted a fraction to me, cupped carefully like espresso distilled from centuries of rainfall.

When I reach the Rue des Jardins again, the dripping paragraph has restarted. Same window, same shy globule of Atlantic, same reluctant farewell. This time, I let the drop complete its journey without cameras. When it falls, I catch it on the Lorde’s open page. The ink blooms a small black flower. I underline it. Annotate: “First rainfall of Mariam.” I name it after the woman who once told me that sorrow is just rainwater learning to walk upright.

Behind me, La Patrone is locking up. She sees the page, the ink bleeding, and says nothing. Only her eyes soften, like edges of stamps licked before journeys. I nod, close the book. The café lights dim. Somewhere, trains across undiscovered archipelagos are sounding their whistles, voices made of iron petals.

I step into the night soft as forgetting. Rain has gone, but the sidewalk gleams like rehearsed reminiscence. Inside my pocket, the amaranth petals hum the children’s hymn of somewhere far yet reachable. I walk north, then whichever direction gravity forgives, carrying a sentence that demands answers and a silence generous enough to house them.

One thousand words later, am I any closer to what I wanted to say? The paragraph is still dripping, provisional. Somewhere, Amarine might be halfway through a vineyard dusk, speaking a poem aloud while grape skins darken into temporary eclipses of the sun. Somewhere else, the child with the wounded ukulele has affixed a new string and already forgotten the cracked syllable it once twanged.

I keep walking, relishing the surplus of horizon. Night is long enough for any remaining vowels to find consonants and reach détente. If I meet another puddle, I will stoop and read it for any unauthorized reflections. And if none appear, I will prepend my name to the margins of silence and continue humming softly, a map made of footsteps and espresso rain,全书待续，正如所有 unfinished sentences demand: listen closely; somewhere, they already are answering.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
